quizzes.d: Explore the cradle of civilization — the Nile valley and the Fertile Crescent

q.t: Which ancient wonder was a statue of the sun god Helios located on the Greek island of Rhodes?
a: The Statue of Zeus at Olympia

q.t: What river was central to the development of ancient Egyptian civilization?
Who was the ancient Egyptian god of the afterlife and judge of the dead?
What ancient structure was built as a tomb for Pharaoh Khufu?
The ancient Mesopotamian city-state of Ur was located in which modern-day country?
Which Mesopotamian ruler created one of the earliest known law codes, around 1754 BC?
What writing system was developed by the Sumerians, making it one of the earliest writing systems in the world?
Which Egyptian pharaoh is associated with the monotheistic religious revolution centered on the sun disk Aten?
The Rosetta Stone, key to deciphering Egyptian hieroglyphics, was written in how many scripts?
What material did the ancient Egyptians use to make paper-like sheets for writing?

quizzes.d: From the Athenian democracy to the fall of the Roman Empire

q.t: Which ancient Greek city-state is credited with developing the first democracy?
In what year was Julius Caesar assassinated?
Who was the Greek god of the sea?
What was the name of the series of wars fought between Rome and Carthage?
The Battle of Marathon in 490 BC was fought between the Greeks and which invading force?
Which Roman emperor issued the Edict of Milan in 313 AD, granting religious tolerance throughout the empire?
What Greek philosopher was sentenced to death by drinking hemlock in 399 BC?
In which year did the Western Roman Empire traditionally fall?
Who was the Macedonian king who conquered much of the known world between 336 and 323 BC?
Which Spartan king led the famous defense at Thermopylae in 480 BC?

quizzes.d: Knights, feudalism, plague, and the holy wars of the Middle Ages

q.t: What year did William the Conqueror defeat King Harold II at the Battle of Hastings?
The Black Death that devastated Europe in the mid-14th century was caused by which bacterium?
Which city was the primary objective of the First Crusade, captured by Crusaders in 1099?
What was the Magna Carta, signed in 1215?
a: A declaration of war against France
A document limiting the powers of the English king
A treaty establishing the Holy Roman Empire
A papal bull authorizing the Crusades

q.t: Who was the Muslim military commander who recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187?
In the feudal system, what term described the land granted by a lord to a vassal in exchange for military service?
The Hundred Years' War was fought primarily between which two kingdoms?
a: France and the Holy Roman Empire

q.t: Which French military leader, claiming divine visions, helped turn the tide of the Hundred Years' War for France?
The Great Schism of 1054 permanently divided Christianity into which two main branches?
Which Mongol ruler founded the largest contiguous land empire in history in the early 13th century?

quizzes.d: European voyages that connected the world and reshaped civilizations

q.t: In what year did Christopher Columbus first reach the Americas?
Which explorer was the first to circumnavigate the globe, completing the voyage in 1522?
Vasco da Gama's 1498 voyage established the first direct sea route from Europe to which region?
Which Spanish conquistador conquered the Aztec Empire in the early 16th century?
The Treaty of Tordesillas in 1494 divided newly discovered lands between which two countries?
Which explorer was the first European to reach the southern tip of Africa, calling it the Cape of Storms?
The Columbian Exchange refers to the transfer of plants, animals, and diseases between which two regions?
Which English sea captain completed the second circumnavigation of the globe between 1577 and 1580?
What did Francisco Pizarro conquer on behalf of Spain in the 1530s?
The Northwest Passage, sought by many explorers, was a route connecting which two oceans?

quizzes.t: World War I and World War II
quizzes.d: The two global conflicts that defined the 20th century

q.t: The assassination of which Austro-Hungarian archduke in 1914 triggered World War I?
In what year did World War II end?
Operation Overlord, the Allied invasion of Nazi-occupied France, took place on which date?
Which treaty formally ended World War I in 1919?
Adolf Hitler became Chancellor of Germany in which year?
The Battle of Stalingrad, a turning point on the Eastern Front, ended with the surrender of which German army group?
Which two Japanese cities were targeted by American atomic bombs in August 1945?
What name was given to the German strategy of rapid, overwhelming military attack used at the start of WWII?
The Zimmermann Telegram of 1917, which helped bring the US into WWI, proposed a military alliance between Germany and which country?
Which international military alliance, led by the United States, was created in 1949 as a collective defense pact against the Soviet Union?

quizzes.t: American History: Revolution to Civil War
quizzes.d: From colonial independence to the struggle over slavery and union

q.t: In what year did the United States declare independence from Britain?
Who was the first President of the United States?
The American Civil War began in 1861 with the Confederate attack on which Union military installation?
Which president issued the Emancipation Proclamation in 1863?
The Boston Tea Party in 1773 was a protest against taxation imposed by which country?
What was the name of the defining 1863 Civil War battle fought in Pennsylvania that turned the tide against the Confederacy?
Which document, ratified in 1788, established the framework for the US federal government?
Who led the Confederate Army of Northern Virginia throughout most of the Civil War?
The Louisiana Purchase of 1803, which doubled the size of the US, was bought from which country?
In which year did the American Civil War end with the surrender at Appomattox Court House?

quizzes.d: Imperial China, feudal Japan, the Mughal Empire, and more

q.t: The Great Wall of China was primarily built to protect against invasions from which direction?
Who was the founder of the Mughal Empire in India, who won the First Battle of Panipat in 1526?
Japan's feudal period was dominated by warrior lords called what?
Which Chinese dynasty built the first version of the Great Wall and standardized weights, measures, and the writing system?
The Silk Road connected China with which distant civilization, facilitating trade for centuries?
Which Mughal emperor commissioned the Taj Mahal as a mausoleum for his wife?
Japan's Meiji Restoration began in 1868 and involved the restoration of power from the shogunate to which figure?
The Tang Dynasty of China (618–907 AD) is notable for reaching its cultural peak under which emperor?
Which Indian emperor of the Maurya dynasty converted to Buddhism after the bloody Battle of Kalinga?
The Opium Wars of the 19th century were fought between China and which Western power?

quizzes.d: The French Revolution, Napoleon, the Cold War, and 20th-century independence movements

q.t: The French Revolution began in 1789 with the storming of which famous Parisian fortress?
In what year did the Berlin Wall fall, symbolizing the end of the Cold War division of Europe?
Who led the Indian independence movement using nonviolent civil disobedience against British rule?
Napoleon Bonaparte was finally defeated at which battle in 1815, ending his rule for good?
The Cuban Missile Crisis of 1962 brought the US and USSR closest to nuclear war. It was resolved when the USSR agreed to remove missiles from Cuba in exchange for what US concession?
a: The US would withdraw from South Vietnam
The US would remove missiles from Turkey and pledge not to invade Cuba
The US would lift its trade embargo on Cuba
The US would recognize the Cuban government
